diagrams.title: Stack Memory Layout with Array Representation
diagrams.description: A vertical block diagram showing how a stack is represented in memory using a fixed-size array, with the 'top' variable tracking the last element position
elements: Array container: 100 indexed slots (0-99) shown as horizontal blocks
Top pointer: A vertical arrow labeled 'top' pointing to index position -1 (empty stack)
Index labels: Numbers 0, 1, 2, ... displayed on left side of each block
Data values: Sample elements (10, 20, 30) shown in bottom-up progression
MAX constant: Labeled boundary at top showing MAX=100 capacity
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize the physical memory arrangement and understand how the single 'top' variable controls the entire stack state, crucial for understanding array-based implementation

diagrams.title: Push Operation Step-by-Step Visualization
diagrams.description: A 4-step flowchart showing the push operation: checking overflow, incrementing top, inserting element, and updating stack
elements: Step 1: Decision diamond 'Is top == MAX-1?' with Yes/No paths
Step 2: Rectangle 'top++' showing index increment
Step 3: Rectangle 'stack[top] = value' showing element insertion
Step 4: Arrow returning to stack state showing updated 'top' pointing to new element
Visual annotation: Before state showing empty position, After state showing filled position
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the exact sequence of operations in push, understanding both the success path and overflow condition handling

diagrams.title: Pop Operation Step-by-Step Visualization
diagrams.description: A 4-step process showing element removal from stack: checking underflow, retrieving element, decrementing top, returning value
elements: Step 1: Decision diamond 'Is top == -1?' (underflow check)
Step 2: Rectangle 'element = stack[top]' showing value retrieval
Step 3: Rectangle 'top--' showing index decrement
Step 4: Arrow returning to stack state showing 'top' moved down
Visual annotation: Highlighted element being removed with dotted outline
diagrams.purpose: Students understand that pop returns the top element before removing it, and grasp the underflow condition handling

diagrams.title: Stack Overflow and Underflow Conditions
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison showing boundary conditions: overflow when stack is full (top=MAX-1) and underflow when empty (top=-1)
elements: Left side: Full stack diagram with all MAX blocks filled, 'top' at MAX-1, red 'OVERFLOW' label with X mark
Right side: Empty stack diagram with no elements, 'top' at -1, red 'UNDERFLOW' label with X mark
Error message boxes: 'Stack Overflow: Cannot push, stack is full' and 'Stack Underflow: Cannot pop, stack is empty'
Boundary markers: Horizontal lines at index -1 and MAX-1
diagrams.purpose: Students recognize critical error conditions and understand why boundary checks are mandatory in stack operations

diagrams.title: Complete Stack Operations Architecture
diagrams.description: Unified diagram showing all four stack operations (Push, Pop, Peek, IsEmpty) with their input, processing, and output
elements: Four operation boxes arranged horizontally: Push(value), Pop(), Peek(), IsEmpty()
Input arrows: value arrow into Push, none into Pop/Peek/IsEmpty
Process labels inside each: 'Check condition, Modify top, Access/Modify array'
Output arrows: 'Success/Value' from Push/Pop/Peek, 'True/False' from IsEmpty
State representation: Array with elements and current top position shown below operations
diagrams.purpose: Provides holistic view of stack as an Abstract Data Type with multiple operations working on the same underlying array structure

charts.title: Stack Operations Time and Space Complexity
charts.content: Table showing Push, Pop, Peek, and IsEmpty operations with their Time Complexity (Worst, Average, Best) and Space Complexity. Push: O(1) all cases, Pop: O(1) all cases, Peek: O(1) all cases, IsEmpty: O(1) all cases. Space Complexity: O(MAX) for the array, O(1) auxiliary space for top pointer

charts.title: Stack Operations Summary Table
charts.content: Four-column table: Operation Name | Function Signature | Algorithm Summary | Return Value. Push: void push(int) | Check overflow, increment top, store value | Nothing. Pop: int pop() | Check underflow, store top value, decrement top | Removed element. Peek: int peek() | Check underflow, return stack[top] | Top element without removal. IsEmpty: bool isEmpty() | Return top == -1 | Boolean indicating empty status

charts.title: Push Operation Flowchart
charts.content: Start → Check if top == MAX-1? → YES → Display 'Overflow' → End. NO → top = top + 1 → stack[top] = value → Display 'Pushed successfully' → End. With clear diamond decision node and rectangular process nodes connected by arrows

charts.title: Pop Operation Flowchart
charts.content: Start → Check if top == -1? → YES → Display 'Underflow' → End. NO → element = stack[top] → top = top - 1 → Display element → End. Shows how returned element flows through the process

branches: Data Structure: Fixed-size array (MAX elements), Linear sequential memory
Key Variable: top pointer (-1 for empty, 0 to MAX-1 for elements)
Core Operations: Push (insert at top), Pop (remove from top), Peek (view top), IsEmpty (check empty)
Time Complexity: O(1) for all operations - constant time
Space Complexity: O(MAX) for array, O(1) auxiliary
Advantages: Simple implementation, No memory wastage, Fast operations
Disadvantages: Fixed capacity, Memory may be underutilized, Cannot dynamically grow/shrink
Applications: Expression evaluation, Function call stack, Undo mechanisms, Backtracking algorithms
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting implementation details (array, top pointer) with operations, complexity analysis, trade-offs, and real-world applications - helping students see the complete picture of array-based stack implementation
